the post-REQUEST_CHANGES retry observes the same Harness pin as the
initial attempt.

AC2 — Same test asserts Model, Profile, and Provider propagation across
the review-driven reopen.

AC3 — All five reopen sources are covered:
  - VerdictRequestChanges → TestExecuteLoopRetryReusesWorkerHarness
  - VerdictBlock          → TestExecuteLoopRetryReusesWorkerHarness_BlockReopen
  - no_changes            → TestExecuteLoopRetryNoChangesReopen
  - land_conflict         → TestExecuteLoopRetryLandConflictReopen
  - post_run_check_failed → TestExecuteLoopRetryPostRunCheckFailedReopen

AC4 — TestExecuteLoopRetryReusesWorkerHarness_NoPinAutoRoutes is the
negative control: worker spawned without pins → all captured pins remain
empty across the reopen-driven retry.

AC5 — `cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/ -run TestExecuteLoopRetry
-count=1` passes (all six tests). Full `go test ./... -count=1` has two
preexisting unrelated failures at base rev 82902a25:
  - cmd/installation_acceptance_test.go TestInstallationPerformance/installation_on_1mbps
    (network-throughput-dependent installation test)
  - internal/agent/agent_test.go TestIntegration_CodexEcho
    (requires the codex CLI; codex returns "thread ... not found")
Neither involves execute-loop retry / pin propagation; both fail on the
clean base before this attempt.

## Production wiring

The fix itself is in cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1796–1840 — the executor
closure passed to ExecuteBeadWorker captures the worker-level `harness`,
`model`, `provider`, `modelRef`, and `effort` from the surrounding
Cobra-flag scope and rebuilds an attemptRcfg from them on every call.
Because Go closures capture by reference, every retry iteration reads
the same pin values the worker was launched with. The mirror wiring is
in cli/internal/server/workers.go.

## Why this attempt is no_changes

The bead is in_progress because prior reviewer attempts errored with
review-error: provider_empty (a reviewer-side failure, not a worker
defect — the result_rev was successfully merged in both prior attempts).
Reopening the bead has not surfaced any additional implementation work
that would advance the AC. Adding speculative code or duplicate tests
would violate DOWITYTD.

## What a follow-up attempt would need

Either (a) the reviewer's provider_empty error is fixed at the reviewer
layer so the post-merge review can produce a verdict and close the bead,
or (b) the bead is force-closed with a note pointing at the existing
test file and production wiring above. Neither is execute-loop work.",

rationale: Bead ddx-ce7a796e (B22d-g) cannot be landed in isolation against base
41d464b03c4e5d12b7a9244c3c59ee312e64e255. Same fundamental blocker as
B22d-f (ddx-9f4b1a8c, no_changes 2026-04-27): lefthook `runtime-lint`
hook (lefthook.yml) filters analyzer diagnostics to the set of staged
files, and the in-scope test files contain pre-existing RunOptions /
CompareOptions composite literals whose underlying APIs are explicitly
named out-of-scope by this bead's parent decomposition (step h owns
service_run.go; runner.go and claude_stream.go aren't in this step's
in-scope at all).

What is done (verified locally, then reverted)
- compare_adapter.go is straightforward to drain of RunOptions /
  CompareOptions / QuorumOptions:
    * RunFunc redeclared as `func(rcfg config.ResolvedConfig,
      runtime AgentRunRuntime) (*Result, error)`.
    * defaultResolvePromptForCompare(runtime AgentRunRuntime).
    * runCompareArmWith(run RunFunc, rcfg, runtime CompareRuntime,
      armIdx, harnessName, baseDir, prompt, worktreePath).
    * RunCompareWith(run, rcfg, runtime CompareRuntime, resolvePrompt,
      cleanupSandbox).
    * RunQuorumWith(run, rcfg, runtime QuorumRuntime).
    * RunBenchmarkWith(runCompare func(config.ResolvedConfig,
      CompareRuntime) (*ComparisonRecord, error), suite).
    * RunCompareViaService / RunCompareWithAgent / RunQuorumViaService /
      RunQuorumWithAgent / RunBenchmarkViaService / RunBenchmarkWithAgent
      rewritten to take (rcfg, runtime) instead of CompareOptions /
      QuorumOptions and to delegate through the new RunFunc.
    * RunCompareWithConfigViaService / RunQuorumWithConfigViaService
      collapsed into a single forwarding hop (they no longer need to
      assemble a CompareOptions/QuorumOptions intermediary).
    * RunQuorumWithConfig retained, now calling RunQuorumWith with the
      new shape.
- models.go BenchmarkArmsToCompare changes return shape from
  CompareOptions to CompareRuntime (and accepts an AgentRunRuntime
  baseline rather than a RunOptions). compare_adapter.go's
  RunBenchmarkWith uses that shape directly.
- models_test.go updates the BenchmarkArmsToCompare call to pass
  `AgentRunRuntime{Prompt: "test"}` and assert
  CompareRuntime.{Harnesses, ArmModels, ArmLabels}. This is the only
  RunOptions-related drift in models_test.go — the rest of that file
  doesn't reference RunOptions.
- After the in-package edits, `cd cli && go build ./...` passes and
  `cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/...` is clean once the test
  files in the next bullet are also updated.
- agent_test.go (out of scope) needs one closure-shape update at
  line 584 because RunFunc's signature changed; with that one-line
  diff applied locally, TestQuorumRunsAllHarnesses passes.

What is blocking (lint policy vs. bead scope)

1. claude_stream_test.go (in-scope) contains RunOptions composite
   literals at lines 206, 212, 218, 224, 291, and 324. These are all
   inside test cases that call helpers declared in claude_stream.go —
   resolveClaudeProgressLogDir(opts RunOptions, cfg Config) at
   claude_stream.go:284 and runClaudeStreamingFn(r, ctx, harness,
   "claude", "", opts RunOptions, "hi", "", 10*time.Second) at
   claude_stream.go:304/477/507. claude_stream.go is NOT in this
   bead's in-scope; B22d's per-step decomposition reserves it for a
   separate step. With claude_stream.go's RunOptions surface
   unchanged, the test cannot construct anything other than
   RunOptions{...} to drive resolveClaudeProgressLogDir, and the
   runtime-lint hook flags every one of those literals when the
   file is staged.

2. prompt_ingress_oversize_test.go (in-scope) has three RunOptions
   composite literals:
     * line 78  — r.resolvePrompt(RunOptions{PromptFile: fixture})
                  drives Runner.resolvePrompt in runner.go:976.
                  runner.go is out of scope.
     * line 85  — defaultResolvePromptForCompare(RunOptions{...})
                  drives the in-scope helper. CAN be updated.
     * line 112 — RunViaServiceWith(ctx, svc, dir, RunOptions{...})
                  drives service_run.go:104. service_run.go's public
                  API is explicitly named out-of-scope ("step h").
   Even after migrating line 85 and updating line 98's RunBenchmarkWith
   closure to the new (rcfg, runtime CompareRuntime) shape, the file
   still carries lines 78 and 112 as pre-existing RunOptions
   composite literals. runtime-lint scopes diagnostics by file (not
   by hunk), so the staged file fails the gate on lines that this
   bead is not authorized to touch.

3. agent_test.go (out of scope, but transitively required) — once
   RunFunc's signature changes, agent_test.go:584's
   `func(opts RunOptions) (*Result, error)` no longer matches
   RunQuorumWithConfig's RunFunc parameter, so the package fails to
   build. The minimal fix is one line, but agent_test.go has 18
   other RunOptions composite literals (BuildArgs call sites at
   lines 118, 130, 148, 157, 165, 188, 196, 209, 214, 677, 684,
   718, 727, 856, 864, 971, plus 678, 684) that are pre-existing,
   unrelated to this bead, and depend on BuildArgs (runner.go) being
   migrated separately. Staging agent_test.go would surface every
   one of those as a lint violation.

The runtime-lint hook is mandatory (per project policy: --no-verify is
forbidden). Diagnostic filtering is whole-file, not hunk-aware, so
"only edit my own lines" doesn't help when the file already contained
violations that this bead's named scope can't reach.

What a follow-up attempt would need (any of the following)

A. Re-decompose B22d-g to also include claude_stream.go (so
   resolveClaudeProgressLogDir / runClaudeStreamingFn migrate off
   RunOptions in the same commit, draining claude_stream_test.go),
   AND service_run.go's RunViaServiceWith + runner.go's
   resolvePrompt (so prompt_ingress_oversize_test.go drains), AND
   agent_test.go's BuildArgs call sites (so the RunFunc signature
   change compiles cleanly across the package). This is essentially
   the previously-attempted atomic B22d scope (worker-3f7a's
   recommendation, applied 2026-04-26 in commit 97561a6e); the
   subsequent re-decomposition into steps a-h reintroduced this
   exact tension.

B. Land step h FIRST (and in fact most of the rest of the
   per-file decomposition) so that claude_stream.go, runner.go,
   service_run.go, and agent_test.go no longer contain pre-existing
   RunOptions usages, then run step g — at that point the in-scope
   test files have no out-of-scope violations and lint passes on the
   staged set. The current bead-queue ordering puts step g before
   step h, so this requires a tracker reordering.

C. Change runtime-lint's filter from whole-file to hunk-scoped (so
   pre-existing violations on lines this bead doesn't touch don't
   block the commit). This is a lint-policy change in lefthook.yml;
   not in this bead's scope and not authorized by the bead
   description.

D. Authorize the worker (model-side) to extend in-scope to cover
   the transitively-required files (claude_stream.go,
   runner.go.resolvePrompt + BuildArgs, service_run.go.RunViaServiceWith,
   and agent_test.go), the same way B22d itself was authorized in
   2026-04-26 (commit 97561a6e). The diff for that expansion is
   already prepared and verified locally; only the bead-scope
   constraint stands in the way.

The implementation is mechanical and verified locally; the blocker is
project-level lint policy versus per-step bead scope, not
implementation difficulty. No partial commit is justified, since
landing only the in-scope-clean half (compare_adapter.go +
models.go + models_test.go) breaks the package build by leaving
agent_test.go with an incompatible RunFunc closure, and updating
that closure trips the lint on agent_test.go's pre-existing 18+
RunOptions literals.

NO CHANGES — bead preconditions not satisfied; scope exceeds single-window budget.

## What is done

- Read the bead, SD-024, TD-024, runtimelint analyzer, and current state of
  cli/internal/agent/types.go and runner.go.
- Surveyed actual caller surface in the worktree (HEAD = 562f293c).

## Why no commit was made

The bead description states explicit dependencies:

  "Depends on bead 16 (Stage 1 cleanup) AND beads 17-21 (every Stage 2
   caller migrated)."

The bead's own reopen note (2026-04-26) confirms those dependencies are
NOT satisfied:

  "type RunOptions still exists at cli/internal/agent/types.go:36;
   QuorumOptions at :81; CompareOptions at :171. runtimelint reports
   ~80 violations across the tree using these types. Closure was fake
   (already_satisfied or premature). The retirement was NOT actually
   performed — only ExecuteBeadLoopOptions (B16) and ExecuteBeadOptions
   (B28) shipped real type-deletion commits."

So B17-B21 (the per-caller Stage 2 migration beads) were never done.
Today's measured surface, from this worktree:

  rg "RunOptions|CompareOptions|QuorumOptions" cli/  →  188 references
                                                        across 48 files
  rg "RunOptions\{|CompareOptions\{|QuorumOptions\{" cli/
                                                     →  ~50 composite literals
                                                        (~13 production,
                                                         ~31 in tests,
                                                         ~5 in lint testdata,
                                                         rest in agent
                                                         package internals)

Production constructor sites:
  cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1462
  cli/internal/agent/compare_adapter.go:61, 261, 414, 685
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:647
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:531
  cli/internal/agent/grade.go:169
  cli/internal/agent/service_run.go:82
  cli/internal/exec/store.go:201
  cli/internal/server/server.go:1913, 3786

Internal-implementation surface (beyond the two files the bead names as
"in-scope"): every dispatch helper in the agent package threads
RunOptions through its signature —

  agent_runner.go, agent_runner_service.go, claude_stream.go,
  compare_adapter.go, execute_bead.go, routing_metrics.go, runner.go,
  script.go, service_run.go, session_index.go, virtual.go

plus BuildArgs, RunAgent, runVirtualFn, runScriptFn,
runClaudeWithFallbackFn, finalizeClaudeResult, processResult, and
TestProviderConnectivity all take RunOptions or fields from it.
CompareOptions and QuorumOptions both EMBED RunOptions, so deleting
RunOptions also forces a redesign of compare_adapter.go's option flow.

## What is blocking

The bead is framed as a one-shot type-deletion ("delete the structs,
rename RunWithConfig → Run"), but the AC requires `go build` and
`go test` to pass tree-wide. To pass those gates the bead has to
also:

1. Replace every internal RunOptions parameter (agent package
   helpers) with an AgentRunRuntime + ResolvedConfig pair, OR keep
   them on a non-RunOptions internal type.
2. Migrate all ~13 production caller sites to RunWithConfig
   (renamed Run), including building a config.ResolvedConfig at each
   site (CLI, server, exec store, grade, compare_adapter,
   execute_bead, execute_bead_review, service_run).
3. Migrate or delete the CompareOptions/QuorumOptions surface
   (compare_adapter.go is heavily built around it; this is essentially
   a rewrite of the comparison/quorum dispatcher, not a rename).
4. Migrate ~31 test composite literals across agent_test.go,
   claude_stream_test.go, prompt_ingress_oversize_test.go,
   session_index_test.go, models_test.go, virtual_test.go,
   plus runtime tests.
5. Update or remove evidencelint analyzer entries for RunOptions
   and the runtimelint testdata (which deliberately uses the legacy
   types as fixtures).

This is the SD-024 §"Migrate one production caller per bead" / §"Migrate
test files in groups" work — the ~5 beads (B17–B21) the bead description
points at as completed dependencies. Doing all of that here in a single
execute-bead window violates the SD's own per-bead size budget
("<500 LoC of net change, <20 minutes of agent time, under $10 of model
spend") by an order of magnitude. The realistic LoC delta is in the
1500–2500 range across 30+ files.

## What a follow-up attempt would need

Reopen B17–B21 (or their successor beads) with concrete per-file scopes
before re-queuing this bead. Suggested split:

- B17: migrate cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go RunViaService caller +
       cli/internal/server/server.go (2 sites). Add a
       config.ResolvedConfig handoff at each site.
- B18: migrate cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go +
       execute_bead_review.go + service_run.go (3 sites; agent-package
       internal callers).
- B19: migrate cli/internal/agent/compare_adapter.go and
       cli/internal/agent/grade.go — biggest surface; redesigns
       CompareOptions/QuorumOptions on top of AgentRunRuntime
       (likely promotes Harnesses/Strategy/Threshold/ArmModels/
       ArmLabels/Sandbox/KeepSandbox/PostRun onto a new
       CompareRuntime/QuorumRuntime).
- B20: migrate cli/internal/exec/store.go + types.go.
- B21: migrate the ~31 test composite literals; update
       evidencelint analyzer + testdata.

Once those land green, this bead (B22) becomes the trivially-correct
final cleanup — delete the three types, rename RunWithConfig → Run,
update the runtimelint testdata that intentionally uses the legacy
names, and confirm runtimelint reports zero violations on the live
tree.

Recommendation: do NOT escalate this bead to a stronger model yet.
The blocker is the missing prior beads, not model capability. A
stronger model attempting the full ~2000-LoC migration in one
worker window would produce churn that is hard to review and likely
breaks one of the three production dispatch sites in a non-obvious
way (the historical bug class SD-024 was created to prevent).",

The bead's acceptance criteria are already fully satisfied by code present at
base revision 83b98c1c3e1c43e2e131635eef19f0861760439c (the execute-bead
starting point for this attempt). The bead was re-queued with stale REVIEW:BLOCK
findings from a prior attempt, but those findings have already been addressed
by commits that are part of base:

  - 7133371f fix: run plugin dispatches as workers [ddx-7de0ce80]
  - efd5bacb fix: refresh plugin cards after dispatch [ddx-7de0ce80]
  - 92e12099 fix(plugins): skip plugin refresh on worker failure phases [ddx-7de0ce80]

Mapping from AC items to implementation at base:

AC1 (per-card in-flight tracking):
  cli/internal/server/frontend/src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/plugins/+page.svelte
  lines 56-60: dispatchingPlugins Set, inFlightWorkers Map<name,workerId>,
  inFlightActions, dispatchSnapshots, workerFailures.
  lines 115-117: isBusy(name) gate.
  lines 458-477: Install/Update buttons disabled while busy.

AC2 (worker-driven refresh):
  +page.svelte lines 225-238 (subscribeToWorker), 240-258 (handleWorkerTerminal).
  On non-failure terminal phase, refreshPlugins() runs, then clearInFlight(name).

AC3 (fallback polling):
  +page.svelte lines 190-223. scheduleFallbackPolling sets a 30s timer
  (configurable via __ddxPluginFallbackDelayMs). When it fires without a
  terminal event, startFallbackPolling polls every 2s until the snapshot
  changes. Playwright test at e2e/plugins.spec.ts:349 ("plugin update falls
  back to polling when worker stream drops terminal event") exercises this.

AC4 (in-flight visual state):
  +page.svelte lines 377-383. Loader2 spinner + "Installing..."/"Updating..."
  label replaces the status badge while workerId && action are set. Button
  disabled with Tooltip pointing to worker.

AC5 (failure surfacing):
  +page.svelte lines 392-405. workerFailures Map stores {workerId, action}
  on failure phases; renders red AlertCircle badge with link to worker page
  and title "Update failed"/"Install failed". handleWorkerTerminal (line 247)
  returns early on failure phases without calling refreshPlugins, preserving
  pre-dispatch state. dispatchError remains for synchronous mutation errors
  only (lines 287-289, 338-343).

AC6 (concurrent dispatch):
  Map-keyed tracking allows independent state per plugin. Header at
  +page.svelte lines 318-335 iterates inFlightWorkers.entries() and renders
  one link per active worker. Playwright test at e2e/plugins.spec.ts:381
  ("two plugin updates track separate workers concurrently").

AC7 (no extra refetch overhead):
  refreshPlugins (+page.svelte:184-188) calls only PLUGINS_LIST_QUERY, not
  the full page data loader.

AC8 (Playwright e2e):
  cli/internal/server/frontend/e2e/plugins.spec.ts has dedicated tests
  covering in-flight state (line 284 "plugin update refreshes card when
  worker completes successfully"), failure (line 314), polling fallback
  (line 349), concurrent (line 381). Uses page.routeWebSocket to simulate
  the workerProgress subscription.

AC9 (no regressions):
  Install flow through ConfirmDialog uses the same dispatchPlugin() code
  path (lines 295-301), so install also gets in-flight tracking.
  Existing install test at e2e/plugins.spec.ts:199 (US-098.b) still asserts
  the Install flow.

Backend wiring:
  cli/internal/server/graphql_adapters.go:119-145 — adapter uses
  WorkerManager.StartPluginAction with an executor callback.
  cli/internal/server/workers.go:393-519 (StartPluginAction + runPluginAction)
  — emits a "running" event at start and a "done" or "failed" phase event
  when DispatchPluginAction returns. Verified by
  TestWorkerManagerStartPluginActionPublishesTerminalProgress and
  TestWorkerManagerStartPluginActionPublishesFailureProgress
  (cli/internal/server/workers_test.go:47,93). Both pass locally.

Verification run in this worktree:
  - `go build ./...` in cli/: clean.
  - `go test ./internal/server/ -run 'Plugin' -v`: both worker tests PASS.

Verdict: no_changes. The bead is multi-surface and the AC set cannot be
completed to the specified quality in a single pass without committing
partial or red code across several surfaces. Per the bead instructions
("a well-justified no_changes is better than a bad commit"), this attempt
returns for breakdown/re-queue with a stronger budget or a pre-split bead set.

What the AC demands (11 items, summarized):

  1. GraphQL resolver executeBeadWorktrees(projectId) returning
     {path, branch, beadId?, executionId?, baseRev, headRev, state,
     createdAt, lastActivityAt} with unit tests for all four states.
  2. Pure, table-driven state classifier
     (active | completed | abandoned | orphan) with ≥2 edge cases per state.
  3. New SvelteKit route /nodes/<n>/projects/<p>/worktrees — sortable,
     filterable table.
  4. Row actions (Remove, Force-remove) wired through GraphQL mutations
     with confirm-gating and typed confirmation for force-remove.
  5. CLI: `ddx server state worktree-prune [--dry-run] [--project] [--yes]`
     with an integration test against a fixture repo containing one of
     each state.
  6. Server-side sweep with configurable interval (default 15m), a
     GraphQL counter rollup type, and opt-in `worktrees.auto_prune` config.
  7. Cross-links to /executions/<id> (depends on ddx-726bc5f2) and a
     reciprocal "Worktree" section on the execution detail page.
  8. Fixture seed with ≥100 worktrees across states; p95 ≤ 200ms.
  9. Playwright e2e that seeds one of each state, asserts state badges,
     exercises Remove confirm flow, and asserts active-worktree gating.
 10. Design note in docs/helix/02-design/ for Part 4 canonical path
     convention + migration plan (required only if Part 4 ships).
 11. Cross-reference notes to ddx-12cae4dd, ddx-71fbe968, ddx-726bc5f2,
     ddx-15f7ee0b.

What is in place already (checked):

  - Server package cli/internal/server/ with graphql resolver module,
    state.go / state_executions.go, workers.go, and a land_coordinator
    — the sweep / state-classification pattern has prior art here.
  - Frontend SvelteKit tree includes /nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]
    with executions, workers, beads, documents, etc. No worktrees route yet.
  - Executions detail page already exists at .../executions/[executionId].
    Cross-link target in AC 7 is therefore implementable, not blocked on
    ddx-726bc5f2 (which may itself be partial or separate).
  - Two worktree path conventions confirmed at runtime:
      .claude/worktrees/agent-<hash>/   (branch-based, older)
      /tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-<bead>-<ts>-<hash>/ (detached)
    This execution itself is running from the /tmp convention.

What is blocking a single-pass completion:

  - The surface area spans Go (resolver + mutations + sweep + classifier +
    CLI subcommand + config plumbing), SvelteKit (new route, table with
    sort/filter, two confirm dialogs, mutation wiring, Houdini type
    regeneration), fixtures (≥100-worktree seeder), unit tests, an
    integration test, and a Playwright e2e. Each surface has its own
    test-green bar; shipping them all together green within one pass
    risks red commits on the surfaces that get squeezed last.
  - AC 5 (CLI subcommand) requires a new `ddx server state` command
    group; the current CLI surface exposes `ddx server` but does not
    have a `state` subcommand tree — adding one is a small but
    independent surface that interacts with the wider command layout.
  - AC 6 (sweep + counter + config) needs config-schema changes
    (.ddx/config.yaml worktrees block), which touches the config loader
    and every test that loads config. This is a cross-cutting change
    and deserves its own attempt slot.
  - AC 8's ≥100-worktree fixture is cheap to generate but the p95
    claim must be measured against a real handler — per the user's
    standing perf stance (feedback_testing_and_performance), a perf
    claim requires baseline + boundary + harness, not just a seed and
    a one-shot timing.

Recommended breakdown (for re-queue):

  A. Inventory resolver + pure classifier + unit tests.
     (AC 1, AC 2; pure, no UI, no config changes.)
  B. Worktrees route + list view + cross-links to executions.
     (AC 3, AC 7; depends on A; no mutations yet.)
  C. Remove / Force-remove mutations + row actions + Playwright e2e.
     (AC 4, AC 9; depends on B.)
  D. CLI worktree-prune subcommand + integration test.
     (AC 5; can run parallel to C once A is done.)
  E. Sweep + counter + config + opt-in auto_prune.
     (AC 6; depends on A; config-surface change.)
  F. ≥100-worktree fixture + perf harness with p95 measurement.
     (AC 8; depends on A; separate because of the perf methodology bar.)
  G. Path-convention design note + Part 4 migration plan.
     (AC 10, AC 11 for cross-refs; docs-only.)

A follow-up attempt should take A alone, or A+B together, to stay
within a single pass's green-bar budget.

Summary

All acceptance criteria for this bead are already satisfied at HEAD by prior merged commits:

  99033c89  fix: index agent sessions by monthly shard [ddx-2ceb02fa]
  4a721780  fix(perf): align agentSessions target with date-range GraphQL signature [ddx-2ceb02fa]
  d3b16629  test: lock session index project writer [ddx-2ceb02fa]

No additional code changes are required in this pass. The REVIEW:BLOCK entries
appended to the bead notes describe the original (pre-amendment) acceptance
text — in particular they assume a flat `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.index.jsonl`
while the amendment ("2026-04-22 — User-directed amendment: shard the index")
explicitly supersedes that with monthly shards under
`.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl`. The current code follows the
amended shape.

What is done (AC-by-AC evidence against the amended contract)
-------------------------------------------------------------
AC 1 (amended) — Sharded index writer.
  cli/internal/agent/session_index.go
    - AppendSessionIndex(): single-writer; shard resolved from time.Now().UTC()
      via SessionIndexShardPath → sessions/sessions-YYYY-MM.jsonl.
    - SessionIndexEntry: pointer-only fields (no prompt/response/stderr bodies).
  Evidence test: TestSessionIndexWritesMonthlyShards (crosses a month boundary
  and asserts two shards).

AC 2 — Single-call-site guarantee.
  cli/internal/agent/service_run.go:225-226
    - RunViaServiceWith calls AppendSessionIndex once per invocation.
  cli/internal/agent/session_index_test.go
    - TestRunViaServiceWithAppendsOneSessionIndexRow: one call → one index row.
    - TestProductionAgentExecutionPathsUseIndexedServiceWriter: enumerates
      execute-bead, execute-loop, `ddx agent run`, quorum/compare, replay,
      and the service writer itself; fails if any path is not wired through
      RunViaService / RunViaServiceWith.

AC 3 — Visibility restored (GraphQL reads the index).
  cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:630-690
    - GetAgentSessionsGraphQL → readProjectSessions → agent.ReadSessionIndex.
  Evidence tests (internal/server): TestGraphQLAgentSessions covers the
  resolver happy path; the e2e spec below asserts the live path.

AC 4 — Body lazy-load resolver.
  cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:648-671
    - GetAgentSessionGraphQL(id) calls agent.FindSessionIndex + LoadSessionBodies.
  GraphQL schema:
    cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql adds `agentSession(id: ID!)`
    with `prompt`, `response`, `stderr` fields.
  Evidence tests:
    - TestLoadSessionBodiesFromBundle (loads prompt/response/stderr from
      `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/` bundle artefacts).
    - e2e/sessions.spec.ts asserts the detail query fires on row click and
      shows bundle prompt/response in the expanded row.

AC 5 (amended) — Legacy migration shards too.
  cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1164-1177
    - `ddx agent log reindex` subcommand.
  cli/internal/agent/session_index.go:398-444
    - ReindexLegacySessions: splits entries by entry.Timestamp into monthly
      shards, dedupes on id, renames the legacy file to
      sessions.jsonl.legacy on first run so the server never reads it again.
  Evidence tests:
    - TestReindexLegacySessionsSplitsShardsAndDedupes (entries across
      Jan/Feb/Mar produce 3 shards; re-run count is 0).
    - TestAgentLogReindexMigratesLegacyFile (cmd-level integration).

AC 6 (amended) — Server reads the shard directory with date-range pushdown;

  cli/internal/agent/session_index.go:371-396
    - SessionIndexShardFiles filters shards by [StartedAfter, StartedBefore).
  Evidence test: TestSessionIndexShardFilesSelectsOnlyIntersectingDateRange

  cli/cmd/agent_usage.go:191-284, 498-518

  cli/cmd/status.go:173-179

  Evidence tests: TestAgentUsage*, TestStatus* (all green).

AC 7 — Perf sanity: sharded read ≤50ms for 12×10k fixture.
  cli/internal/agent/session_index_test.go:241-313
    - BenchmarkReadSessionIndexDefaultWindow: 12 shards × 10k rows each,
      default window (current + previous shard).
    - BenchmarkLegacySessionsJSONLFullScan: legacy single-file full scan

    - Environment footer: linux/arm64, Go go1.26.2.

AC 8 — UI gap banner.

AC 9 — Playwright e2e.

would unwind the amendment that the bead owner explicitly requested and would
reintroduce the single-file aggregate they asked us to split. That is not a
correct change. A no_changes attempt with the evidence above is the right
signal here.

against the amended acceptance criteria (notes section, 2026-04-22 amendment
— supersedes original AC 1/5/6/7). The amendment documents exactly why the
index is sharded under `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions/` instead of a flat
`sessions.index.jsonl`.",

.ddx/executions/20260425T214601-82538bfc/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Report
External report at /tank/home/erik/Downloads/ddx-agent-run-invalid-timeout-exits-zero.md claims `ddx agent run --timeout 60ss ...` exits 0 on bad input, breaking wrapper exit-code inference (canonical caller: helix CLI).

## Verification
Bug does NOT reproduce on current main. cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:117-120 already returns invalid-timeout error from RunE; cli/main.go:31-43 propagates non-nil cobra errors to exit code 1. Reporter is on stale niflheim/sindri host installs.

## Real gaps
1. No regression test guards the exit-code semantic. cli/cmd/agent_run_profile_test.go only exercises happy-path `--timeout 3s`.
2. SilenceUsage is not set on `agent run` — flag parse errors dump the entire usage block above the error message (the cosmetic part of the reporter's complaint).
3. `agent doctor` (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:647) calls time.ParseDuration on its own --timeout flag and shares the same class of bug. Same one-line fix.

## Plan
(a) Set `SilenceUsage: true` on `newAgentRunCommand` (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:91 area) and on `newAgentDoctorCommand` (sibling, ~line 647). Per-command scope, not root — matches existing precedent (cli/cmd/init.go:99 sets SilenceUsage only; cli/cmd/doc.go:89,118 set both). Do NOT set SilenceErrors=true; keep cobra's stderr error line.

(b) Add a regression test asserting `agent run --timeout 60ss --harness codex --text "..."` returns a non-nil err from executeCommand whose message contains `invalid timeout`. This is sufficient to catch the regression because main.go:31-43 turns any non-nil err into exit 1 — no subprocess test needed. Place the test in a neutrally named file, not agent_run_profile_test.go (the profile-specific filename is misleading for a generic flag-parse test).

(c) Optional polish: assert the captured output after SilenceUsage does NOT contain `Usage:`/`Flags:` lines.

(d) Do NOT extend coverage to other duration-typed inputs across the CLI; out of scope for this bug.

## Out of scope
- Helix-side fix (already done at /home/erik/Projects/helix/scripts/helix line 70 per reporter).
- Root-level cobra UX changes.
- Validation hardening of other duration flags elsewhere.

## Refinement log
Reviewed by fresh-eyes (general-purpose subagent) and codex (`ddx agent run --harness codex`). Fresh-eyes verified main.go propagates cobra errors faithfully — command-level test is sufficient. Codex pushed for a subprocess exit-code test; rejected because main.go:31 invariant makes the command-level assertion equivalent. Both reviewers agreed per-command SilenceUsage is the right scope (matches init.go/doc.go precedent). Folded in: doctor-command parity, SilenceErrors warning, test-file placement note. Rejected: broader CLI sweep, subprocess test.",
.ddx/executions/20260418T005000-cfeffe49/manifest.json:15:    "description": "## Problem

`ddx agent workers` lists running workers but provides no way to stop them.
Workers can hang indefinitely (today observed: 9 workers running 33h–142h on
agent projects). Operators have no clean way to terminate them — they have to
hunt for the process manually (`ps aux | grep claude`) and `kill -9`, which
bypasses any cleanup the worker might perform (temp files, worktree cleanup,
bead state updates).

Workers tracked in JSON today expose only: id, kind, state, bead_id, harness,
model, started_at, attempts, failures. No PID is surfaced, so external tooling
cannot cleanly target them either.

## Proposed shape

```
ddx agent workers stop <worker-id>        # graceful stop (SIGTERM, then SIGKILL after grace)
ddx agent workers stop --all-over 1h      # stop every running worker older than 1h
ddx agent workers stop --state running    # stop all running workers
ddx agent workers stop --bead <bead-id>   # stop the worker assigned to a bead
```

Graceful path:
1. Send SIGTERM to worker process group
2. Wait configurable grace period (default 30s)
3. Send SIGKILL if still alive
4. Mark worker state as 'stopped' (distinct from 'failed' and 'exited')
5. Run worker cleanup hook (worktree teardown, bead state revert if still claimed)

Also surface PID in `ddx agent workers --json` so external tooling can target
processes directly when the CLI path is unavailable.

## Related

Observed on agent project 2026-04-17: nine workers running 33h–142h with no
bead assigned; root cause unclear. Even if the root cause gets fixed, operators
need a supported way to reap them.

## Files likely touched

- cmd/agent/workers.go — add stop subcommand and pid JSON field
- internal/agent/worker/registry.go — surface pid; add Stop() with graceful-then-force semantics
- internal/agent/worker/process.go — process group signaling, grace timer
- worker state machine — add 'stopping' and 'stopped' states",

.ddx/executions/20260425T090214-6d2682c9/result.json:3:  "rationale": "**Config-to-loop wiring missing.** `NewConfig.ReviewMaxRetries` and `ResolveReviewMaxRetries()` exist (cli/internal/config/types.go), and `ExecuteBeadLoopOptions.ReviewMaxRetries` is honored (cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go), but no caller in `cli/cmd/` reads the resolved value and sets it on the options struct. `grep -r ReviewMaxRetries cli/cmd` returns nothing. AC #4 explicitly requires "verifies the loop respects the override" sourced from `.ddx/config.yaml`; the existing config test stops at the loader. Add the plumbing in the `ddx work` / `ddx agent execute-loop` command wiring and either extend `TestReviewMaxRetriesConfig` to drive the loop via that path, or add an integration test that loads YAML→options→worker and asserts escalation at the configured threshold."
.ddx/executions/20260418T021319-5c32287b/manifest.json:15:    "description": "## Problem

RouteRequest struct at cli/internal/agent/types.go:330-337 has no Provider field:

    type RouteRequest struct {
        Profile         string // cheap, standard, smart
        ModelRef        string // logical catalog ref or alias
        ModelPin        string // exact concrete model string
        Effort          string
        Permissions     string
        HarnessOverride string
    }

NormalizeRouteRequest (cli/internal/agent/routing.go:305-312) only translates --provider when it names a known harness (or alias like 'local'); otherwise it DROPS the value entirely:

    } else if flags.Provider != '' {
        resolved := resolveHarnessAlias(flags.Provider)
        // Only treat as harness override if it names a registered harness;
        // otherwise it's a ddx-agent provider name (e.g. 'vidar') and
        // should not constrain harness selection.
        if _, ok := builtinHarnesses[resolved]; ok {
            req.HarnessOverride = resolved
        }
        // else: provider silently discarded — RouteRequest carries no provider preference
    }

Result: the rest of the routing engine (candidate planning, scoring, escalation) has no way to see --provider. The --provider flag 'works' today only because RunOptions.Provider is passed directly to resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider at agent_runner.go:93 via a parallel code path that bypasses routing.

This is the root cause of every gap SD-015 calls 'provider soft preference' — Mode 2 step 3 ('pick best provider'), Mode 3 ('provider soft preference'), Mode 5 ('--provider P alone'). None can work without a Provider field in RouteRequest.

## Fix

.ddx/executions/20260418T021319-5c32287b/prompt.md:5:## Problem

RouteRequest struct at cli/internal/agent/types.go:330-337 has no Provider field:

    type RouteRequest struct {
        Profile         string // cheap, standard, smart
        ModelRef        string // logical catalog ref or alias
        ModelPin        string // exact concrete model string
        Effort          string
        Permissions     string
        HarnessOverride string
    }

NormalizeRouteRequest (cli/internal/agent/routing.go:305-312) only translates --provider when it names a known harness (or alias like 'local'); otherwise it DROPS the value entirely:

    } else if flags.Provider != '' {
        resolved := resolveHarnessAlias(flags.Provider)
        // Only treat as harness override if it names a registered harness;
        // otherwise it's a ddx-agent provider name (e.g. 'vidar') and
        // should not constrain harness selection.
        if _, ok := builtinHarnesses[resolved]; ok {
            req.HarnessOverride = resolved
        }
        // else: provider silently discarded — RouteRequest carries no provider preference
    }

Result: the rest of the routing engine (candidate planning, scoring, escalation) has no way to see --provider. The --provider flag 'works' today only because RunOptions.Provider is passed directly to resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider at agent_runner.go:93 via a parallel code path that bypasses routing.

This is the root cause of every gap SD-015 calls 'provider soft preference' — Mode 2 step 3 ('pick best provider'), Mode 3 ('provider soft preference'), Mode 5 ('--provider P alone'). None can work without a Provider field in RouteRequest.

## Fix

1. Add Provider string field to RouteRequest (types.go:330). Document its semantics: 'preferred provider name (soft affinity for candidate ranking; hard affinity when combined with --harness or when it's the only input)'.

2. Update NormalizeRouteRequest (routing.go:305-312) to ALWAYS capture flags.Provider into req.Provider regardless of harness naming. Keep the existing harness-alias resolution separately.

3. Teach scoreCandidate (routing.go:471-550) to award a bonus when the candidate's concrete provider matches req.Provider (SD-015 'Provider as Modifier' soft preference).

4. Teach BuildCandidatePlans (routing.go:27+) to preserve the discovered provider on each CandidatePlan so scoreCandidate has the data to do the comparison.

5. Regression test: RouteRequest{Provider: 'vidar-omlx', ModelPin: 'Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit'} produces a candidate plan that ranks vidar-omlx (embedded-openai surface) above other providers with the same model, and CandidatePlan carries Provider='vidar-omlx'.

## Files likely touched

- cli/internal/agent/types.go — RouteRequest + CandidatePlan.Provider (CandidatePlan already has Provider per earlier work)
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go — NormalizeRouteRequest, scoreCandidate, evaluateCandidate (for assigning Provider to plans)
- cli/internal/agent/routing_test.go — Provider propagation + soft-preference scoring tests
- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — ensure --provider reaches both RouteFlags.Provider and RunOptions.Provider as today

## Depends on

None. This is the foundational fix; everything else in the epic depends on it.

.ddx/executions/20260418T030326-2b94449b/manifest.json:14:    "description": "## Problem

No single document traces the provider/model/harness resolution path from CLI flags to the final HTTP request. SD-015 and SD-023 are SPECS — they describe what should happen in 5 routing modes. The code lives across routing.go, discovery.go, agent_runner.go, runner.go, and agent_cmd.go. A newcomer debugging a routing bug must infer the flow by reading all of them.

The audit for epic ddx-2d974641 turned up a RouteRequest struct missing its Provider field, and a parallel RunOptions.Provider path that bypasses routing — neither of which is documented. This is the kind of hidden structural defect a Resolution Path document would have flagged on first read.

## Fix

Write docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (or similar location; coordinate with SD-015's author). The document traces one concrete invocation end-to-end:

    ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model-ref qwen/qwen3.6

Walk through every layer, with file:line citations:

1. **CLI parse** — cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go flag extraction (line references).

2. **RouteFlags -> RouteRequest normalization** — routing.go:NormalizeRouteRequest. What each CLI flag maps to; which flags are discarded when they don't match an expected shape; the current gap around --provider.

3. **Discovery probe** — discovery.go:DiscoverProviderModels. When it fires; what it caches; the 30s TTL; how failures are handled.

4. **Candidate planning** — routing.go:BuildCandidatePlans / evaluateCandidate. For each registered harness, the state map lookup and the viability checks.

5. **Fuzzy matching** — discovery.go:FuzzyMatchModel. Pool selection (global vs per-provider), normalization (current gaps), prefix + tiebreak algorithm.

6. **Scoring** — routing.go:scoreCandidate. Per-profile scoring, cost class, historical success rate, provider-affinity bonus (when implemented).

7. **Candidate ranking** — routing.go:RankCandidates / SelectBestCandidate. Non-viable rejection, stable tiebreak.

8. **Dispatch** — runner.go:Run. Harness-specific branches (virtual, agent, script, HTTP-provider-embedded, default exec). For embedded: agent_runner.go:RunAgent -> resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider -> agent library call.

9. **Wire-level request** — ddx-agent builds the HTTP body (system_prompt + messages + tools), sends to the provider. What the server receives.

10. **Response parsing** — streaming or non-streaming decode; where 'unexpected end of JSON input' (ddx-6a5dfe35) surfaces.

Include a SEQUENCE DIAGRAM (mermaid or ASCII) showing the layers.

Include a GAP TABLE at the top listing every currently-missing piece (as of whatever the SD-015 implementation state is) with links to the specific beads tracking each gap.

Keep the document MAINTAINED alongside the code: update it when RouteRequest gains a field, when a new routing mode lands, when fuzzy match gains a normalization step.

## Files likely touched

- docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (new)
- docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-agent-service.md — add a single-line pointer at the top of the 'Overview' section
- skills/ddx/reference/agents.md — add a one-line pointer in 'Harness / profile / persona dispatch' section so agents consulting the skill can follow up to the full trace when needed

## Out of scope

- Rewriting SD-015 or SD-023 — this doc complements them; they describe intent, this describes current reality.
- Documenting future routing modes that haven't landed.",

.ddx/executions/20260418T045141-e59897a9/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Root cause RC1 from ddx-0a651925

`cli/internal/agent/runner.go:239` (Runner.Run) and `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:163` (Runner.RunAgent) both call `context.WithCancel(context.Background())`, discarding any context from the caller. `RunOptions` exposes no ctx field. `agent.ExecuteBead` likewise takes no ctx, so `server.WorkerManager.runWorker`'s ctx never reaches the agent call.

As a result, `WorkerManager.Stop(id)` (server/workers.go:648) cancels only the worker goroutine; the embedded `agentlib.Run` call (and any subprocess it launched) keeps running until SIGKILL. This is the structural defect behind 33–142h hung workers.

## Scope

1. Add `Context context.Context` to `RunOptions` (or change signatures to `Run(ctx, opts)`). Default to `context.Background()` when nil.
2. Derive the internal ctx from the caller's rather than `context.Background()`.
3. Thread ctx through `agent.ExecuteBead` → `runner.Run` → `agentlib.Run` → provider HTTP.
4. Update `server.WorkerManager.runWorker`'s `singleTierAttempt` closure to pass `ctx` into `agent.ExecuteBead`.

## Non-goals

- New wall-clock timeout logic (covered by sibling RC2 bead)
- Operator-facing stop command (covered by ddx-b808df39)
",

.ddx/executions/20260418T051525-40ea409f/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Root cause RC2 from ddx-0a651925

The `DefaultTimeoutMS = 7200000` (2h) in `cli/internal/agent/types.go:363` is advertised as a hard cap but implemented as an *idle timeout*:

- `cli/internal/agent/executor.go:154-178` resets `time.NewTimer(idleTimeout)` on every stdout/stderr byte.
- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:264-285` resets on every non-compaction agentlib event.

A provider that emits any heartbeat, retry line, or periodic event defeats the timer indefinitely — enabling the 33–142h hangs observed on agent projects.

Only the claude streaming path (`cli/internal/agent/claude_stream.go:373-384`) uses a true wall-clock deadline via `time.After(timeout)`. That path is why bundle `20260415T015231-c2ffa9ba` fired at exactly 2h 0m 0.098s — and nothing else does.

## Scope

1. Add an absolute wall-clock deadline in addition to (not replacing) the idle timeout.
2. Default the wall-clock bound to something operators can trust — e.g. 3x `DefaultTimeoutMS` or a new `DefaultWallClockMS`.
3. Apply in: `OSExecutor.ExecuteInDir` (subprocess path) and `Runner.RunAgent` (embedded agent path).
4. Reflect the wall-clock error in result.json as a distinct `error: "wall-clock deadline exceeded after Xh"` so it is not confused with idle timeout.

## Non-goals

- Lowering or removing the existing idle timeout.
- Per-provider tuning (can be follow-up).",
.ddx/executions/20260418T051525-40ea409f/manifest.json:14:    "acceptance": "- [ ] New default wall-clock deadline (e.g. 3h) applied to both `OSExecutor.ExecuteInDir` and `Runner.RunAgent`
- [ ] Wall-clock timeout fires even when stdout/stderr/agent events are flowing (unit test: fake provider emits 1 event/sec for > deadline, expect cancel at deadline ± 1s)
- [ ] Idle timeout behaviour preserved (unit test: no activity for idle timeout → fire)
- [ ] Result.json distinguishes `timeout: wall-clock` from `timeout: idle`
- [ ] Documented in types.go (DefaultWallClockMS constant with comment)",

.ddx/executions/20260418T054825-291242ac/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Residual risk after RC1–RC4 from ddx-0a651925

Even with context propagation (RC1), wall-clock bounds (RC2), tightened breakers (RC3), and provider deadlines (RC4), a goroutine-level deadlock or panic-swallowed handler could still leave a worker alive past any reasonable bound. Today, `server.WorkerManager.Stop` (workers.go:648) only calls `handle.cancel()` — it has no escalation to OS-level signal and no autonomous trigger. ddx-b808df39 adds the *operator* tool; this bead adds the *autonomous* watchdog.

## Scope

1. WorkerManager runs a supervisor goroutine that, every minute, inspects each `workerHandle`.
2. If `time.Since(record.StartedAt) > WatchdogDeadline` (default e.g. 6h) AND `record.CurrentAttempt` has not transitioned in `StallDeadline` (default e.g. 1h), escalate:
   - Send SIGTERM to the worker's process group (requires surfacing PID, aligned with ddx-b808df39).
   - After 30s grace, send SIGKILL.
   - Mark record.State = "reaped"; update claimed bead state so the claim is not leaked.
3. Emit a `bead.reaped` event on the tracker with the full timeline so post-mortems are easy.

## Non-goals

- Per-bead runtime tuning; defaults are enough.
- Replacing ddx-b808df39 — this is defense-in-depth, not duplication. ddx-b808df39 is operator-initiated; this bead is system-initiated.

## Dependencies

- ddx-b808df39 surfaces PID in `ddx agent workers --json` output; this bead reuses that plumbing.",

.ddx/executions/20260418T063750-16fb3982/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Problem

No single document traces the provider/model/harness resolution path from CLI flags to the final HTTP request. SD-015 and SD-023 are SPECS — they describe what should happen in 5 routing modes. The code lives across routing.go, discovery.go, agent_runner.go, runner.go, and agent_cmd.go. A newcomer debugging a routing bug must infer the flow by reading all of them.

The audit for epic ddx-2d974641 turned up a RouteRequest struct missing its Provider field, and a parallel RunOptions.Provider path that bypasses routing — neither of which is documented. This is the kind of hidden structural defect a Resolution Path document would have flagged on first read.

## Fix

Write docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (or similar location; coordinate with SD-015's author). The document traces one concrete invocation end-to-end:

    ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model-ref qwen/qwen3.6

Walk through every layer, with file:line citations:

1. **CLI parse** — cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go flag extraction (line references).

2. **RouteFlags -> RouteRequest normalization** — routing.go:NormalizeRouteRequest. What each CLI flag maps to; which flags are discarded when they don't match an expected shape; the current gap around --provider.

3. **Discovery probe** — discovery.go:DiscoverProviderModels. When it fires; what it caches; the 30s TTL; how failures are handled.

4. **Candidate planning** — routing.go:BuildCandidatePlans / evaluateCandidate. For each registered harness, the state map lookup and the viability checks.

5. **Fuzzy matching** — discovery.go:FuzzyMatchModel. Pool selection (global vs per-provider), normalization (current gaps), prefix + tiebreak algorithm.

6. **Scoring** — routing.go:scoreCandidate. Per-profile scoring, cost class, historical success rate, provider-affinity bonus (when implemented).

7. **Candidate ranking** — routing.go:RankCandidates / SelectBestCandidate. Non-viable rejection, stable tiebreak.

8. **Dispatch** — runner.go:Run. Harness-specific branches (virtual, agent, script, HTTP-provider-embedded, default exec). For embedded: agent_runner.go:RunAgent -> resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider -> agent library call.

9. **Wire-level request** — ddx-agent builds the HTTP body (system_prompt + messages + tools), sends to the provider. What the server receives.

10. **Response parsing** — streaming or non-streaming decode; where 'unexpected end of JSON input' (ddx-6a5dfe35) surfaces.

Include a SEQUENCE DIAGRAM (mermaid or ASCII) showing the layers.

Include a GAP TABLE at the top listing every currently-missing piece (as of whatever the SD-015 implementation state is) with links to the specific beads tracking each gap.

Keep the document MAINTAINED alongside the code: update it when RouteRequest gains a field, when a new routing mode lands, when fuzzy match gains a normalization step.

## Files likely touched

- docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (new)
- docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-agent-service.md — add a single-line pointer at the top of the 'Overview' section
- skills/ddx/reference/agents.md — add a one-line pointer in 'Harness / profile / persona dispatch' section so agents consulting the skill can follow up to the full trace when needed

## Out of scope

- Rewriting SD-015 or SD-023 — this doc complements them; they describe intent, this describes current reality.
- Documenting future routing modes that haven't landed.",
.ddx/executions/20260418T063750-16fb3982/manifest.json:14:    "acceptance": "1. docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md exists and documents all 10 layers above with file:line citations current as of the document's creation date.

2. The document contains a sequence diagram showing CLI -> NormalizeRouteRequest -> Discovery -> BuildCandidatePlans -> SelectBestCandidate -> Run -> RunAgent -> agent library -> HTTP.

3. The document contains a 'Current Gaps' table with at least the 4 confirmed gaps from epic ddx-2d974641 and links to the tracking beads.

4. FEAT-006 and skills/ddx/reference/agents.md each have a one-line pointer to the new doc.

5. **CI grep verifies citations resolve** (per opus fresh-eyes review). A CI step (or lefthook hook) parses every file:line citation in SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md and verifies the named symbol/line pattern exists in the current source tree. Prevents the doc from rotting silently as routing.go / discovery.go / types.go evolve. Failing citations block the PR.",

.ddx/executions/20260418T064756-b495509a/prompt.md:484:+    "description": "## Problem

No single document traces the provider/model/harness resolution path from CLI flags to the final HTTP request. SD-015 and SD-023 are SPECS — they describe what should happen in 5 routing modes. The code lives across routing.go, discovery.go, agent_runner.go, runner.go, and agent_cmd.go. A newcomer debugging a routing bug must infer the flow by reading all of them.

The audit for epic ddx-2d974641 turned up a RouteRequest struct missing its Provider field, and a parallel RunOptions.Provider path that bypasses routing — neither of which is documented. This is the kind of hidden structural defect a Resolution Path document would have flagged on first read.

## Fix

Write docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (or similar location; coordinate with SD-015's author). The document traces one concrete invocation end-to-end:

    ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model-ref qwen/qwen3.6

Walk through every layer, with file:line citations:

1. **CLI parse** — cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go flag extraction (line references).

2. **RouteFlags -> RouteRequest normalization** — routing.go:NormalizeRouteRequest. What each CLI flag maps to; which flags are discarded when they don't match an expected shape; the current gap around --provider.

3. **Discovery probe** — discovery.go:DiscoverProviderModels. When it fires; what it caches; the 30s TTL; how failures are handled.

4. **Candidate planning** — routing.go:BuildCandidatePlans / evaluateCandidate. For each registered harness, the state map lookup and the viability checks.

5. **Fuzzy matching** — discovery.go:FuzzyMatchModel. Pool selection (global vs per-provider), normalization (current gaps), prefix + tiebreak algorithm.

6. **Scoring** — routing.go:scoreCandidate. Per-profile scoring, cost class, historical success rate, provider-affinity bonus (when implemented).

7. **Candidate ranking** — routing.go:RankCandidates / SelectBestCandidate. Non-viable rejection, stable tiebreak.

8. **Dispatch** — runner.go:Run. Harness-specific branches (virtual, agent, script, HTTP-provider-embedded, default exec). For embedded: agent_runner.go:RunAgent -> resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider -> agent library call.

9. **Wire-level request** — ddx-agent builds the HTTP body (system_prompt + messages + tools), sends to the provider. What the server receives.

10. **Response parsing** — streaming or non-streaming decode; where 'unexpected end of JSON input' (ddx-6a5dfe35) surfaces.

Include a SEQUENCE DIAGRAM (mermaid or ASCII) showing the layers.

Include a GAP TABLE at the top listing every currently-missing piece (as of whatever the SD-015 implementation state is) with links to the specific beads tracking each gap.

Keep the document MAINTAINED alongside the code: update it when RouteRequest gains a field, when a new routing mode lands, when fuzzy match gains a normalization step.

- docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (new)
- docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-agent-service.md — add a single-line pointer at the top of the 'Overview' section
- skills/ddx/reference/agents.md — add a one-line pointer in 'Harness / profile / persona dispatch' section so agents consulting the skill can follow up to the full trace when needed

## Out of scope

- Rewriting SD-015 or SD-023 — this doc complements them; they describe intent, this describes current reality.
- Documenting future routing modes that haven't landed.",

.ddx/executions/20260418T064927-78e29296/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Problem (filed per codex + opus fresh-eyes review 2026-04-17)

Both reviews flagged the same missing concept: routing currently scores candidates on cost class, quota, and historical success rate — but does NOT gate on whether the resolved route can actually execute the requested work. Concrete failure modes this lets through:

1. **Context-window overflow.** Bead prompt is 80k tokens. Fuzzy match picks a 32k-context model on the pinned provider. Routing 'succeeds'. Dispatch fails mid-stream with a provider 400 or truncated output.

2. **Missing tool-calling support.** Some omlx flavors or older local servers don't implement /v1/chat/completions with tools. Routing picks them via fuzzy match. Dispatch fails with 'tools not supported' or silently returns a no-tool response that the execute-bead loop can't use.

3. **Missing structured-output support.** Some provider/model combos don't implement JSON-mode / tool-use-required. If the bead prompt relies on it, routing-level blindness means the failure only surfaces at dispatch.

4. **Effort / permissions.** --effort high or --permissions unrestricted may not be honored by every harness+provider combination. Routing should reject or downgrade rather than dispatch and fail.

Codex: 'routing currently scores mostly on cost/quota/history, not whether a provider/model can satisfy tool calling, effort controls, permissions, or context-window needs.'
Opus: 'If the bead prompt is 80k tokens and the fuzzy-matched model is 32k, routing succeeds and dispatch fails mid-stream. Omlx flavor may not support tools at all.'

## Out of scope for this bead

This bead tracks the CONCEPT as a sibling of the main autorouting epic, but a full capability-constraint scoring system is a larger change than the current Phase-1 scope. Per opus: 'Scope this out of the epic explicitly (don't expand), but add a sibling bead so it's tracked.'

Split out into sub-scope bites is TBD:

1. **Context-window gating** — smallest useful slice. RouteRequest gains an optional EstimatedPromptTokens field; evaluateCandidate rejects (or penalizes) candidates whose concrete_model's known context window is below that. Uses v0.3.11's LookupModelLimits + catalog ContextWindow fallback.

2. **Tool-calling support gating** — needs per-provider / per-flavor capability metadata. Likely populated from discovery (/v1/models/status) + hand-curated capability table for harnesses.

3. **Effort / permissions gating** — mostly already in place for harnesses (Harness.EffortFlag checked at routing.go:rejectIfNotViable) but provider-specific gating (e.g., 'openrouter doesn't honor effort on non-reasoning models') is not.

4. **Structured-output support** — probably lowest priority; most reasoning-tool-capable models also do structured output.

This bead's AC is to file the sub-beads (smaller, focused) and close THIS bead once the sub-beads exist. The epic ddx-2d974641 does NOT depend on those sub-beads for its exit gate — context-window etc. are a Phase 2 concern. The epic is about correct resolution; capability gating is about preventing resolved-but-unrunnable routes.

## Files touched (by sub-beads, not this one)

- cli/internal/agent/types.go — CandidatePlan capability fields, RouteRequest capability hints
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go — capability checks in rejectIfNotViable + scoring
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go — ContextWindow, supports_tools, supports_structured_output per ModelEntry/Surface",

.ddx/executions/20260418T065645-2b07ca41/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Problem

`ddx agent workers` lists running workers but provides no way to stop them.
Workers can hang indefinitely (today observed: 9 workers running 33h–142h on
agent projects). Operators have no clean way to terminate them — they have to
hunt for the process manually (`ps aux | grep claude`) and `kill -9`, which
bypasses any cleanup the worker might perform (temp files, worktree cleanup,
bead state updates).

Workers tracked in JSON today expose only: id, kind, state, bead_id, harness,
model, started_at, attempts, failures. No PID is surfaced, so external tooling
cannot cleanly target them either.

## Proposed shape

```
ddx agent workers stop <worker-id>        # graceful stop (SIGTERM, then SIGKILL after grace)
ddx agent workers stop --all-over 1h      # stop every running worker older than 1h
ddx agent workers stop --state running    # stop all running workers
ddx agent workers stop --bead <bead-id>   # stop the worker assigned to a bead
```

Graceful path:
1. Send SIGTERM to worker process group
2. Wait configurable grace period (default 30s)
3. Send SIGKILL if still alive
4. Mark worker state as 'stopped' (distinct from 'failed' and 'exited')
5. Run worker cleanup hook (worktree teardown, bead state revert if still claimed)

Also surface PID in `ddx agent workers --json` so external tooling can target
processes directly when the CLI path is unavailable.

## Related

Observed on agent project 2026-04-17: nine workers running 33h–142h with no
bead assigned; root cause unclear. Even if the root cause gets fixed, operators
need a supported way to reap them.

## Files likely touched

- cmd/agent/workers.go — add stop subcommand and pid JSON field
- internal/agent/worker/registry.go — surface pid; add Stop() with graceful-then-force semantics
- internal/agent/worker/process.go — process group signaling, grace timer
- worker state machine — add 'stopping' and 'stopped' states",

.ddx/executions/20260418T191746-684f612a/manifest.json:15:    "description": "## Problem

RouteRequest struct at cli/internal/agent/types.go:330-337 has no Provider field:

    type RouteRequest struct {
        Profile         string // cheap, standard, smart
        ModelRef        string // logical catalog ref or alias
        ModelPin        string // exact concrete model string
        Effort          string
        Permissions     string
        HarnessOverride string
    }

NormalizeRouteRequest (cli/internal/agent/routing.go:305-312) only translates --provider when it names a known harness (or alias like 'local'); otherwise it DROPS the value entirely:

    } else if flags.Provider != '' {
        resolved := resolveHarnessAlias(flags.Provider)
        // Only treat as harness override if it names a registered harness;
        // otherwise it's a ddx-agent provider name (e.g. 'vidar') and
        // should not constrain harness selection.
        if _, ok := builtinHarnesses[resolved]; ok {
            req.HarnessOverride = resolved
        }
        // else: provider silently discarded — RouteRequest carries no provider preference
    }

Result: the rest of the routing engine (candidate planning, scoring, escalation) has no way to see --provider. The --provider flag 'works' today only because RunOptions.Provider is passed directly to resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider at agent_runner.go:93 via a parallel code path that bypasses routing.

This is the root cause of every gap SD-015 calls 'provider soft preference' — Mode 2 step 3 ('pick best provider'), Mode 3 ('provider soft preference'), Mode 5 ('--provider P alone'). None can work without a Provider field in RouteRequest.

## Fix

1. Add Provider string field to RouteRequest (types.go:330). Document its semantics: 'preferred provider name (soft affinity for candidate ranking; hard affinity when combined with --harness or when it's the only input)'.

2. Update NormalizeRouteRequest (routing.go:305-312) to ALWAYS capture flags.Provider into req.Provider regardless of harness naming. Keep the existing harness-alias resolution separately.

3. Teach scoreCandidate (routing.go:471-550) to award a bonus when the candidate's concrete provider matches req.Provider (SD-015 'Provider as Modifier' soft preference).

4. Teach BuildCandidatePlans (routing.go:27+) to preserve the discovered provider on each CandidatePlan so scoreCandidate has the data to do the comparison.

5. Regression test: RouteRequest{Provider: 'vidar-omlx', ModelPin: 'Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit'} produces a candidate plan that ranks vidar-omlx (embedded-openai surface) above other providers with the same model, and CandidatePlan carries Provider='vidar-omlx'.

## Files likely touched

- cli/internal/agent/types.go — RouteRequest + CandidatePlan.Provider (CandidatePlan already has Provider per earlier work)
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go — NormalizeRouteRequest, scoreCandidate, evaluateCandidate (for assigning Provider to plans)
- cli/internal/agent/routing_test.go — Provider propagation + soft-preference scoring tests
- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — ensure --provider reaches both RouteFlags.Provider and RunOptions.Provider as today

## Depends on

None. This is the foundational fix; everything else in the epic depends on it.",

2. NormalizeRouteRequest captures flags.Provider into req.Provider in all cases (including when it doesn't name a harness).
3. scoreCandidate applies a soft-preference bonus when candidate's discovered provider matches req.Provider. Regression test asserts the ranking flip.
4. 'ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx' emits a routing event on the bead recording {resolved_provider: 'vidar-omlx', route_reason: 'provider-affinity', ...} — proving provider preference flows end-to-end.
5. cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/ passes.

6. **--harness × --provider interaction** (per codex + opus fresh-eyes review). NormalizeRouteRequest MUST spec the precedence explicitly:
   - --harness alone: pin harness. Provider ignored.
   - --provider alone: pin provider; routing picks best harness whose candidate plan includes that provider.
   - --harness X --provider Y where X's surface is compatible with Y: pin BOTH. (Compatible = Y is a discovered provider whose /v1/models or equivalent serves a model usable by X.)
   - --harness X --provider Y where Y is NOT compatible with X (e.g., --harness codex --provider vidar-omlx — codex is a cloud subscription harness, vidar-omlx is a local openai-compat endpoint): fail fast at normalization with a clear diagnostic ('harness X does not dispatch to provider Y; --harness and --provider are incompatible for this combination'). Do NOT silently drop one flag.
   - Regression test covers all four cases plus the incompatible-combo fail-fast path.",

.ddx/executions/20260418T191746-684f612a/prompt.md:8:1. types.go:RouteRequest has a Provider field with documented semantics.
2. NormalizeRouteRequest captures flags.Provider into req.Provider in all cases (including when it doesn't name a harness).
3. scoreCandidate applies a soft-preference bonus when candidate's discovered provider matches req.Provider. Regression test asserts the ranking flip.
4. 'ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx' emits a routing event on the bead recording {resolved_provider: 'vidar-omlx', route_reason: 'provider-affinity', ...} — proving provider preference flows end-to-end.
5. cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/ passes.

6. **--harness × --provider interaction** (per codex + opus fresh-eyes review). NormalizeRouteRequest MUST spec the precedence explicitly:
   - --harness alone: pin harness. Provider ignored.
   - --provider alone: pin provider; routing picks best harness whose candidate plan includes that provider.
   - --harness X --provider Y where X's surface is compatible with Y: pin BOTH. (Compatible = Y is a discovered provider whose /v1/models or equivalent serves a model usable by X.)
   - --harness X --provider Y where Y is NOT compatible with X (e.g., --harness codex --provider vidar-omlx — codex is a cloud subscription harness, vidar-omlx is a local openai-compat endpoint): fail fast at normalization with a clear diagnostic ('harness X does not dispatch to provider Y; --harness and --provider are incompatible for this combination'). Do NOT silently drop one flag.
   - Regression test covers all four cases plus the incompatible-combo fail-fast path.

.ddx/executions/20260425T214601-82538bfc/prompt.md:5:## Report
External report at /tank/home/erik/Downloads/ddx-agent-run-invalid-timeout-exits-zero.md claims `ddx agent run --timeout 60ss ...` exits 0 on bad input, breaking wrapper exit-code inference (canonical caller: helix CLI).

## Verification
Bug does NOT reproduce on current main. cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:117-120 already returns invalid-timeout error from RunE; cli/main.go:31-43 propagates non-nil cobra errors to exit code 1. Reporter is on stale niflheim/sindri host installs.

## Real gaps
1. No regression test guards the exit-code semantic. cli/cmd/agent_run_profile_test.go only exercises happy-path `--timeout 3s`.
2. SilenceUsage is not set on `agent run` — flag parse errors dump the entire usage block above the error message (the cosmetic part of the reporter's complaint).
3. `agent doctor` (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:647) calls time.ParseDuration on its own --timeout flag and shares the same class of bug. Same one-line fix.

## Plan
(a) Set `SilenceUsage: true` on `newAgentRunCommand` (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:91 area) and on `newAgentDoctorCommand` (sibling, ~line 647). Per-command scope, not root — matches existing precedent (cli/cmd/init.go:99 sets SilenceUsage only; cli/cmd/doc.go:89,118 set both). Do NOT set SilenceErrors=true; keep cobra's stderr error line.

(b) Add a regression test asserting `agent run --timeout 60ss --harness codex --text "..."` returns a non-nil err from executeCommand whose message contains `invalid timeout`. This is sufficient to catch the regression because main.go:31-43 turns any non-nil err into exit 1 — no subprocess test needed. Place the test in a neutrally named file, not agent_run_profile_test.go (the profile-specific filename is misleading for a generic flag-parse test).

(c) Optional polish: assert the captured output after SilenceUsage does NOT contain `Usage:`/`Flags:` lines.

(d) Do NOT extend coverage to other duration-typed inputs across the CLI; out of scope for this bug.

## Out of scope
- Helix-side fix (already done at /home/erik/Projects/helix/scripts/helix line 70 per reporter).
- Root-level cobra UX changes.
- Validation hardening of other duration flags elsewhere.

## Refinement log
Reviewed by fresh-eyes (general-purpose subagent) and codex (`ddx agent run --harness codex`). Fresh-eyes verified main.go propagates cobra errors faithfully — command-level test is sufficient. Codex pushed for a subprocess exit-code test; rejected because main.go:31 invariant makes the command-level assertion equivalent. Both reviewers agreed per-command SilenceUsage is the right scope (matches init.go/doc.go precedent). Folded in: doctor-command parity, SilenceErrors warning, test-file placement note. Rejected: broader CLI sweep, subprocess test.

(a) WHAT IS DONE
----------------
The previous attempt (commit 5ae02964) migrated the high-value `r.Run(RunOptions{...})`
and `RunViaService(..., RunOptions{...})` test sites onto the SD-024 successor API
(`r.RunWithConfig` + `RunWithConfigViaService` + `AgentRunRuntime`) across:

  - cli/internal/agent/agent_test.go    (~22 sites: TestRunWith*, TestSessionLogging,
                                         TestRunPromptFile, TestRunStdinMode,
                                         TestRunModel*, TestRunNonZeroExit,
                                         TestRunUnknownHarness, TestRunEmptyPrompt,
                                         TestIntegration_CodexEcho, TestRunOpencode*,
                                         and the *Permissions* pair)
  - cli/internal/agent/runner_test.go   (lmstudio / openrouter dispatch regressions)
  - cli/internal/agent/script_test.go   (runScript helper now builds rcfg via
                                         TestRunConfigOpts and dispatches via
                                         RunWithConfigViaService)

Those 26 sites are the bulk of the "test sites that exercise the high-level agent
run path" called out in the bead description.

(b) WHAT IS BLOCKING
--------------------
The bead AC reads:

    rg 'RunOptions\{|CompareOptions\{|QuorumOptions\{' cli/ returns 0 matches outside
    the type definitions about to be retired.

This is a *global* gate. It cannot be satisfied by test-only changes — it requires
retiring every remaining `RunOptions{...}` construction in both production and test
code. The remaining sites fall into two classes:

CLASS A — tests that call functions whose signature *takes* RunOptions, so the
RunOptions construction is not a "test site" the test could rewrite without
changing the function under test:

  - cli/internal/agent/agent_test.go        BuildArgs(h, RunOptions{...}, model)  — 14 sites
                                            BuildArgs is the function under test.
  - cli/internal/agent/prompt_ingress_oversize_test.go
        line 78  resolvePrompt(RunOptions{PromptFile})              — exercises r.resolvePrompt
        line 85  defaultResolvePromptForCompare(RunOptions{...})    — exercises pkg-level helper
        line 112 RunViaServiceWith(ctx, svc, dir, RunOptions{...})  — exercises stub-injection helper
  - cli/internal/agent/session_index_test.go
        line 195 SessionIndexEntryFromResult(root, RunOptions{...}, ...)
        line 278 RunViaServiceWith(ctx, svc, dir, RunOptions{...})
  - cli/internal/agent/models_test.go
        line 45  BenchmarkArmsToCompare(arms, RunOptions{...})
  - cli/internal/agent/virtual_test.go
        lines 164/174/214 runVirtualFn(runner, RunOptions{...})
  - cli/internal/agent/claude_stream_test.go
        lines 206/212/218/224/291/324  internal opts: RunOptions{...} fixtures

Migrating any of these test sites requires first changing the *signature* of the
production function under test (BuildArgs, resolvePrompt, defaultResolvePromptForCompare,
RunViaServiceWith, runVirtualFn, BenchmarkArmsToCompare, SessionIndexEntryFromResult,
plus the internal claude_stream helpers). That is production-code refactor work,
explicitly outside the scope the bead description names.

CLASS B — production callers still constructing RunOptions{...}:

  - cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go              :1462
  - cli/internal/server/server.go     :1913, :3786
  - cli/internal/exec/store.go        :201
  - cli/internal/agent/runner.go      :248, :281, :300, :321, :976
  - cli/internal/agent/service_run.go :82  (RunWithConfigViaService bridge — by design)
  - cli/internal/agent/compare_adapter.go :61, :261, :414, :685
  - cli/internal/agent/grade.go       :169
  - cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go :706
  - cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go :531

Until these are migrated (and in many cases until the function signatures they
flow through are migrated), the AC's grep gate cannot return zero matches.

The fresh-eyes review on the prior attempt itself concluded:

    (b) remaining *_test.go RunOptions sites,
    (c) production-code migration,

That assessment is correct. The bead title ("5-10 sites") and description ("migrate
test sites … in agent_test.go and any sibling test using RunOptions") describe a
test-scoped refactor; the AC describes a global retirement. The two are not
compatible inside one bead.

(c) WHAT A FOLLOW-UP ATTEMPT WOULD NEED
---------------------------------------
Either:

  Option 1 (preferred): re-cut this bead with a narrowed AC, e.g.

      "rg 'RunOptions\{' cli/internal/agent/{agent,runner,script}_test.go returns
      0 matches in tests that exercise r.Run / RunViaService."

      That AC matches the test-only scope, and the prior commit already satisfies it.

      a multi-file Stage-2 refactor touching cmd/, internal/server/, internal/exec/,
      and most of internal/agent/. It also requires migrating the signatures of
      BuildArgs, resolvePrompt, defaultResolvePromptForCompare, RunViaServiceWith,
      runVirtualFn, BenchmarkArmsToCompare, and SessionIndexEntryFromResult so the
      test sites in Class A can shed RunOptions. This belongs in its own bead (or
      epic), not in a "5-10 sites" test-migration bead.
